text: Time Served

title: West Virginia
text:
1,066
27%
9
In Prison Past TheirParole Eligibility*
Prison Population Incarcerated Past Their Parole Eligibility*
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Most people in prison earn one day of credit for each day served. Credits are deducted from judicial maximum terms to produce earlier mandatory release dates. So-called “indeterminate” sentences carry minimum and maximum terms that are specified in statute and cannot be varied by the court when imposing a prison sentence. There is no standard ratio between minimum and maximum terms. The other sentence type “definite sentences” include terms to parole release eligibility that are set by statutory formula rather than the judicial sentence; the default is parole-release eligibility after 25 percent.







In 2020, there were 1,066 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 27 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 26 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 89 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 91 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 43 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 24 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 71 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for property (46%) and drugs (15%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 74 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.
















“What if we change the section from Missing Data to more of a high-level state-specific Methods section? It can include:

Total N for the state

How we determined parole eligibility (e.g., what formulas did we use to estimate parole eligibility year)

Missing data patterns”


Jess S.







From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 12 percent, changing from 6,681 in 2010 to 5,847 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2022, most people in prison were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 84 percent of people in prison.










In 2022, most people in prison were males, representing 90 percent of people in prison.










In 2020, most people in prison were between 25 to 34 years old, representing 33 percent of people.


















In 2020, most people in prison were incarcerated for property, representing 23 percent of people.















In 2020, most people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 41 percent of people.














From 2010 to 2020, prison releases increased 13 percent, dropping from 2,970 in 2010 to 3,342 in 2020.









In 2020, 45 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 42 percent increase compared to 2010.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 87 percent of prison releases.










In 2020, most people released from prison were males, representing 84 percent of prison releases.










In 2020, most releases from prison were people between 25 to 34 years old, representing 39 percent of prison releases.


















In 2020, most people released from prison were incarcerated for property offenses, representing 38 percent of people.


















Race and EthnicityGender












Black people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 3.9 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in West Virginia.














Race and EthnicityGender










For robbery offenses, Hispanic people spent 4 more years in prison on average compared to people of Other race(s), who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










On average, females spent 1.2 fewer years in prison compared to males in West Virginia.








For murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 6.8 more years in prison on average compared to females.


















Race and EthnicityGender

















Race and EthnicityGender


Hispanic people spent an average of 0.1 more years in prison past parole eligibility compared to White people.

For murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, people of Black, non-Hispanic had 1.9 more years in prison past parole eligibility on average compared to people of White, non-Hispanic people, who had the time served for these offenses.










Females spent an average of 0.4 fewer years in prison past parole eligibility compared to males.
Avg. Years in Prison After Parole Eligibility



2 Years

Male

For murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, females spent 1.5 more years in prison past parole eligibility on average compared to males.


















While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: Utah
text:
532
25%
5
In Prison Past TheirParole Eligibility*
Prison Population Incarcerated Past Their Parole Eligibility*
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Sentencing judges have no discretion to set or influence the actual durations of the prison sentences they impose for each crime. “Judicial” maximum prison sentences must be fixed at the same level as the statutory maximum penalty for the offense of conviction. Any applicable minimum terms to parole-release eligibility are also preset by statute. Parole release dates are determined by the parole board.







In 2020, there were 532 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 25 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 13 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 63 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 90 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 35 to 44 years old, representing 33 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 55 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 33 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault (28%) and property (13%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 64 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.
















“What if we change the section from Missing Data to more of a high-level state-specific Methods section? It can include:

Total N for the state

How we determined parole eligibility (e.g., what formulas did we use to estimate parole eligibility year)

Missing data patterns”


Jess S.







From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 13 percent, changing from 6,807 in 2010 to 5,911 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2022, most people in prison were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 57 percent of people in prison.










In 2022, most people in prison were males, representing 93 percent of people in prison.










In 2020, most people in prison were between 35 to 44 years old, representing 32 percent of people.


















In 2020, most people in prison were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault, representing 28 percent of people.















In 2020, most people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 36 percent of people.














From 2010 to 2020, prison releases increased 34 percent, dropping from 3,108 in 2010 to 4,156 in 2020.









In 2020, 40 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 5 percent increase compared to 2010.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 65 percent of prison releases.










In 2020, most people released from prison were males, representing 86 percent of prison releases.










In 2020, most releases from prison were people between 25 to 34 years old, representing 36 percent of prison releases.


















In 2020, most people released from prison were incarcerated for property offenses, representing 25 percent of people.


















Race and EthnicityGender












Black people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 8.7 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Utah.























Race and EthnicityGender










For negligent manslaughter offenses, Black people spent 13.5 more years in prison on average compared to people of Other race(s), who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










On average, females spent 1 fewer years in prison compared to males in Utah.








For murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 12.1 more years in prison on average compared to females.

title: Tennessee
text:
9,519
52%
7
In Prison Past TheirParole Eligibility*
Prison Population Incarcerated Past Their Parole Eligibility*
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Sentences under 2 years are released to probation automatically upon eligibility. For parole release, there is a statutory staggered percentage formula (generally 30-percent to 60-percent of max) for parole release eligibility differentiated by sentencing “range” based on prior convictions. Starting in 2021 there is a growing list of 100-percent offenses.







In 2020, there were 9,519 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 52 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 15 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 55 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 92 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 35 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 47 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 50 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for drugs (23%) and property (22%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 43 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.
















“What if we change the section from Missing Data to more of a high-level state-specific Methods section? It can include:

Total N for the state

How we determined parole eligibility (e.g., what formulas did we use to estimate parole eligibility year)

Missing data patterns”


Jess S.







From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 20 percent, changing from 27,451 in 2010 to 21,995 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2022, most people in prison were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 56 percent of people in prison.










In 2022, most people in prison were males, representing 91 percent of people in prison.










In 2020, most people in prison were between 25 to 34 years old, representing 31 percent of people.


















In 2020, most people in prison were incarcerated for drugs, representing 21 percent of people.















In 2020, most people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 32 percent of people.














From 2010 to 2020, prison releases decreased 11 percent, dropping from 16,223 in 2010 to 14,517 in 2020.









In 2020, 36 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 2 percent decrease compared to 2010.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 67 percent of prison releases.










In 2020, most people released from prison were males, representing 82 percent of prison releases.










In 2020, most releases from prison were people between 25 to 34 years old, representing 35 percent of prison releases.


















In 2020, most people released from prison were incarcerated for property offenses, representing 31 percent of people.


















Race and EthnicityGender












Black people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 3.8 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Tennessee.














Race and EthnicityGender

In 2020, there were 9,519 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 52 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 15 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 55 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 92 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 35 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 47 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 50 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for drugs (23%) and property (22%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 43 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

title: Tennessee
text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Most people in prison become eligible for discretionary parole release at 25- or 33-percent of maximum with the possibility of somewhat earlier eligibility through credit earnings of 20 days per month. “No parole” crimes are not eligible for parole until they have served 85 percent of the judicial sentence with three days credit per month.







In 2020, there were 4,484 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 45 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 9 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic people, representing 65 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 97 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 39 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 55 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 43 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for robbery (23%) and property (21%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 76 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




There is no traditional good-time or earned time system in Pennsylvania. Pennsylvania judges have discretion to set the durations of minimum terms to parole-release eligibility which can be any length up to 50 percent of the maximum.







In 2020, there were 8,735 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 50 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 19 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 49 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 96 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 38 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 55 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 44 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault (22%) and public order (18%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 56 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: North Dakota
text: Most people in prison are eligible for discretionary parole release soon after they enter prison. The parole board has broad discretion to set minimum terms to parole release consideration for each prisoner individually. Time credits against the maximum sentence top out at five days per month (nine percent). Violent offenses are subject to an 85 percent eligibility rule.

Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




For each of five felony grades, the court’s choice of a maximum prison sentence yields a range of minimum terms the court may select in conjunction within that maximum. The minimum term can be no less than one year and no more than 40 percent of the judicial maximum sentence. Nevada has a multi-layered system of good time and earned time credits which are deducted from incarcerated people’s minimum and maximum terms. The advancement of parole release eligibility is limited to 58 percent of the minimum term.







In 2020, there were 1,903 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 29 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 26 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge

In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 41 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 36 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 63 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for public order (34%) and property (22%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 60 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

For general-rules people in prison, first eligibility for discretionary parole release occurs at the 25-percent mark of incarcerated people’s judicial maximum sentences. Sentencing courts in Montana have the unusual power to order in individual cases that people in prison will never enjoy eligibility for discretionary release or that their minimum terms to release eligibility will be delayed past the 25-percent mark of their maximum sentences. Montana is one of a small number of states that have abolished good time, earned time, and related credits for people in prison to reduce their judicial maximum sentences or the period of time to first parole-release eligibility.







In 2020, there were 395 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 40 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 17 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 77 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 96 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 35 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 64 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 36 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for aggravated or simple assault (29%) and rape or sexual assault (21%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 69 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




The parole board by policy sets parole eligibility for many offenses: A maximum sentence of 40 years or more is eligible in 8 years; maximum terms of two to 39 years are eligible at the 20-percent; and sentences of one year and up to but not including two years are eligible at 4 months. By statute there is a 15 percent group for nonviolent Class D offenses and an 85 percent group for violent offenses.







In 2020, there were 4,665 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 41 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 15 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 70 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 92 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 36 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 56 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 39 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault (22%) and property (18%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 68 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
By statute, most people in prison become eligible for discretionary parole release after serving one-third of their judicial maximum sentences with some exceptions. Georgia is one of four states that have no system of good-time or earned-time credits that can alter the lengths of incarcerated people’s minimum or maximum terms.







In 2020, there were 20,401 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 79 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 15 percent.















Race and EthnicityGenderAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic people, representing 60 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 94 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 38 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 64 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 34 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for aggravated or simple assault (21%) and robbery (20%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 73 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: National Trends
text:
Parole Eligibility by State
This table provides a comprehensive overview of state-level prison populations*, highlighting the percentage and number of people in prison past their parole eligibility year, alongside details about whether the state has abolished discretionary parole and the number of parole board members.







*Includes people in prison for new crimes and with original sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years. Does not include people in prison for parole revocations or sentence lengths less than one year or more than 25 years.

Download Table

title: Arnold Ventures Parole Project
text:
The Council of State Governments (CSG) Justice Center intends to illuminate parole release policies and practices in the United States and how they impact prison population sizes and lengths of stay, using data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) from up to 45 states to:

Examine trends in their parole-eligible populations;
Conduct original empirical research on racial, ethnic, and gender disparities on the portion of sentences that are served in carceral settings past parole eligibility; and
Conduct deep dives into selected states to shed light on parole board decision-making. For these states, we will seek to identify any disparate decision-making and associated outcomes and recommend changes to policy and practice to prevent unnecessarily long prison stays while maintaining public safety.

Sentences of up to 15 years are subject to DOC time credit that can erode 60-70% of the maximum while sentences greater than 15 years have no time credit eligibility and only parole release is possible before discharge.

In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 35 to 44 years old, representing 50 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 3 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 78 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for public order (38%) and drugs (38%) offenses.

Most people in prison become eligible for parole release—called “transfer to community correction”—after serving minimum terms of one-third or one-half of their judicial maximum sentences which may be reduced by “meritorious good time” credits. Substantial changes in release law will take effect in 2025 under Act 659 (2023).







In 2020, there were 369 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 5 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 3 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic people, representing 53 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 95 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 34 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 37 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 51 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for aggravated or simple assault (32%) and property (31%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 47 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

In 2020, 75 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 6 percent decrease compared to 2010.

title: Arkansas
text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Generally, prison sentences include a judicial maximum term but no minimum term. Most people in prison are eligible for discretionary parole release immediately upon admission.







In 2020, there were 2,504 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 59 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 10 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 63 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 95 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 36 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 37 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 44 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for property (25%) and robbery (17%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 66 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

In 2020, there were 2,504 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 59 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 10 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 63 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 95 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 36 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 37 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 44 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for property (25%) and robbery (17%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 66 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

title: Iowa
text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

By statute, fixed maximum terms must be included in most judicial sentences. Minimum terms can be as short as the judge elects but may not exceed two-thirds of the maximum sentence. There is no system of good-time earned-time or similar credits that may be accumulated by people in prison as a discount.

Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Minimum terms to parole release eligibility for general-rules people in prison are not fixed by statute in Missouri but are established by regulations promulgated by the parole board with a range from 15 percent to 33 percent of the maximum. By statute dangerous offenses serve 85 percent and repeated commitments to prison for other serious offenses result in 40, 50, and 70 percent minimums.







In 2020, there were 5,543 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 43 percent of people* in prison.

In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 35 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 65 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 33 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault (24%) and robbery (17%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 66 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

title: Missouri
text: Minimum terms to parole release eligibility for general-rules people in prison are not fixed by statute in Missouri but are established by regulations promulgated by the parole board with a range from 15 percent to 33 percent of the maximum. By statute dangerous offenses serve 85 percent and repeated commitments to prison for other serious offenses result in 40, 50, and 70 percent minimums.

title: Missouri
text:
In 2020, there were 5,543 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 43 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 4 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 58 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 95 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 35 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 65 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 33 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault (24%) and robbery (17%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 66 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




For maximum terms no longer than four years, prison sentences have no parole release eligibility. Maximum terms of 20 years or more almost always include parole release eligibility with reductions for good time credits; this group of people in prison becomes eligible for discretionary parole release after serving one-half of their judicial minimum terms. This means that no matter how long a minimum term the judge may have selected and because the minimum may not exceed the maximum first parole eligibility will always come at the 50-percent mark of the judicial maximum sentence or earlier.







In 2020, there were 54 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 2 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility decreased by 9 percent.

In 2020, 44 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 54 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for property (26%) and public order (19%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 80 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

Most people in prison are serving “determinate” sentences but a substantial number are serving “indeterminate” sentences. Determinate sentences are for higher levels of seriousness and they receive a judicial maximum that can only be reduced with credits topping out at 14 percent discount. Indeterminate sentences receive a judicial minimum and maximum with no statutory constraint on the ratio between them. people in prison serving indeterminate sentences are eligible for parole “at any time” after the expiration of their minimum sentences which may be advanced by as much as 17 percent for merit time credits.







In 2020, there were 10,295 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 52 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 9 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic people, representing 49 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 97 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 38 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 52 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 47 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for robbery (16%) and public order (16%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 40 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

title: Oklahoma
text:
4,957
33%
5
In Prison Past TheirParole Eligibility*
Prison Population Incarcerated Past Their Parole Eligibility*
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




The Oklahoma parole board has authority to grant discretionary parole release only to people in prison convicted of nonviolent offenses. For people in prison convicted of “violent offenses” the parole board is limited only to making recommendations to release to the governor. The release decision itself must be made by the governor; it is the only state where the governor is the ultimate decision-maker in ordinary parole-release cases for large numbers of people in prison. For offenses committed between July 1, 1998 and November 1, 2018 people in prison become eligible after serving one-third of their judicial maximum sentences. For offenses committed on or after November 1, 2018 people in prison become eligible after one-fourth of their judicial maximum terms. people in prison convicted of nonviolent offenses are eligible for more streamlined “administrative parole.” Violent offenses are subject to 85 percent eligibility. Earned credits can reach 60 days per month for nonviolent and 44 days per month for violent.







In 2020, there were 4,957 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 33 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility decreased by 4 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were Unknown people, representing 73 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 91 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 34 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 63 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 31 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault (24%) and robbery (17%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 74 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

title: Rhode Island
text: Most people in prison with sentences of more than six months become eligible for discretionary parole release when they have served one-third of their judicial maximum sentences. people in prison who earn the full credits generally available for good behavior, prison work, and program participation accrue credits at a rate of 17 days per month. Credits are deducted from maximum terms but do not affect parole eligibility minimums.

title: South Dakota
text:
260
17%
9
In Prison Past TheirParole Eligibility*
Prison Population Incarcerated Past Their Parole Eligibility*
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




By statute, there is a continuum of staggered parole-release formulas from 25-percent to 75-percent mark based on offense severity and criminal history. (For crime committed after July 1, 2023, sentences for designated serious crimes have been made non-parolable.) The state also uses an “administrative parole release” system that streamlines the procedural path to release compared with traditional parole frameworks. A large percentage of people in prison in South Dakota are assigned “initial parole dates” shortly after admission with the expectation that they will be released without a hearing before the parole board if they comply with the requirements of their “individual program directives.” Discharge credits could total as much as 270 days per year advancing mandatory release 43 percent.







In 2020, there were 260 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 17 percent of people* in prison.








From 2013 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 11 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were Unknown people, representing 100 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 95 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 39 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 67 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 32 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for aggravated or simple assault (26%) and rape or sexual assault (25%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 60 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

title: Texas
text:
41,644
60%
7
In Prison Past TheirParole Eligibility*
Prison Population Incarcerated Past Their Parole Eligibility*
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Uses two major categories by offense. The lower-severity group become eligible for parole when their time served plus good time credits equals one-quarter of the maximum or 15 years whichever is less. The higher severity group become eligible for parole at one-half half of the maximum or 30 years whichever is less and without reduction by good time. Good time credits are in the 10 to 20 days per month range based on classification. Good time can advance mandatory release but the parole board has discretion to veto mandatory release.







In 2020, there were 41,644 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 60 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 10 percent.

In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 53 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Vermont judges have discretion to vary the relationship between the minimum and maximum prison terms they impose. In most cases both the judicial minimum and maximum are subject to potential reductions through credit earnings. Starting in 2021 earned time credits of 7 days per month reduce both minimum and maximum. Serious “listed” offenses are subject to traditional discretionary parole while unlisted offenses are subject to presumptive more automatic release.

In 2020, most people in prison were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault, representing 25 percent of people.















In 2020, most people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 35 percent of people.














From 2017 to 2020, prison releases decreased 48 percent, dropping from 3,913 in 2017 to 2,018 in 2020.









In 2020, 0 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 0 percent increase compared to 2010.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions.

The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: Wyoming
text:
271
21%
7
In Prison Past TheirParole Eligibility*
Prison Population Incarcerated Past Their Parole Eligibility*
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Sentencing courts have wide discretion to vary the degree of indeterminacy in a sentence with minimums allowed to range up to 90 percent of the maximum. Good time credits can achieve a one-third reduction (15 days/month) in a mandatory release date and advance parole eligibility.







In 2020, there were 271 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 21 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 16 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 80 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 90 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 37 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 50 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 48 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault (34%) and drugs (24%) offenses.















In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 55 percent of in prison past parole eligibility.

From 2010 to 2022, the prison population increased 1 percent, changing from 2,112 in 2010 to 2,123 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2022, most people in prison were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 74 percent of people in prison.










In 2022, most people in prison were males, representing 89 percent of people in prison.










In 2020, most people in prison were between 25 to 34 years old, representing 30 percent of people.


















In 2020, most people in prison were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault, representing 24 percent of people.















In 2020, most people in prison had sentence lengths between 5 to 9.9 years, representing 34 percent of people.














From 2010 to 2020, prison releases increased 41 percent, dropping from 787 in 2010 to 1,110 in 2020.









In 2020, 48 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 11 percent decrease compared to 2010.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 73 percent of prison releases.










In 2020, most people released from prison were males, representing 81 percent of prison releases.










In 2020, most releases from prison were people between 25 to 34 years old, representing 38 percent of prison releases.


















In 2020, most people released from prison were incarcerated for drugs offenses, representing 34 percent of people.


















Race and EthnicityGender












Black people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 5.6 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Wyoming.























Race and EthnicityGender


On average, Black people spent 0.5 more years in prison compared to White people.








For robbery offenses, Black people spent 8 more years in prison on average compared to people of Other race(s), who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










On average, females spent 0.7 fewer years in prison compared to males in Wyoming.








For negligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 3 more years in prison on average compared to females.

title: Georgia
text: Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.

title: National Trends
text:
Parole Eligibility Data by State
This table provides a comprehensive overview of state-level prison populations*, highlighting the percentage and number of people in prison past their parole eligibility year, alongside details about whether the state has abolished discretionary parole and the number of parole board members.







*Includes people in prison for new crimes and with original sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years. Does not include people in prison for parole revocations or sentence lengths less than one year or more than 25 years.
